description: Terran, Vasudan, Shivan, Ancient — factions and their defaults
text:
Species define the major factions in the FreeSpace universe. The four canonical species are:

- **Terran**: Humanity, part of the Galactic Terran-Vasudan Alliance (GTVA).
- **Vasudan**: An alien race, allied with Terrans in the GTVA after the Terran-Vasudan War.
- **Shivan**: A mysterious and hostile alien race, the primary antagonist.

- **Ancient**: An extinct civilization wiped out by the Shivans 8000 years before the events of FreeSpace.

Each species has default settings for IFF (identification friend-or-foe), AWACS multipliers, countermeasures, and support ships. Mods may add additional species, or may even use the species system for custom category distinctions such as Civilian or Pirate.

description: Team allegiances: friend, foe, and neutral relationships
text: IFF determines team allegiances in a mission. Standard IFF teams include "Friendly", "Hostile", and "Unknown", but custom IFF entries can be defined. Each IFF has an associated color for HUD display and relationships with other IFF teams (friend, foe, or neutral). Ships on the same IFF team are almost always allies; ships on different IFF teams may be allies, enemies, or indifferent. Each IFF entry specifies a list of IFFs to be attacked. SEXPs can change IFF allegiances mid-mission.

description: Types (abstract categories) vs. classes (specific ship models)
text: A **ship type** (e.g. "fighter", "bomber", "cruiser", "capital") is an abstract category that defines shared AI behaviors, targeting rules, and gameplay properties. A **ship class** (e.g. "GTF Ulysses", "GVF Serapis") is a specific model of ship with its own stats, weapons, and visual appearance. Each ship class belongs to exactly one ship type via its `class_type` field. Ship types control things like whether the AI will accept player orders, auto-attack enemies, attempt broadside maneuvers, or form into wings.

description: Naming conventions and faction/role prefix codes for ship classes
text:
FreeSpace ship class names usually follow a convention of **operator code** + **classification code**.

**Operators**:
- `T` — Terran
- `V` — Vasudan
- `S` — Shivan
- `GT` — Galactic Terran
- `NT` — Neo-Terran
- `PV` — Parliamentary Vasudan
- `GV` — Galactic Vasudan
- `HL` — Hammer of Light

**Classifications**:
- `F` — Fighter
- `B` — Bomber
- `DR` / `Dr` — Drone
- `FR` / `Fr` — Freighter
- `S` — Support
- `C` — Cruiser or Cargo
- `AC` — Armored Cargo
- `TC` — Tech Cargo
- `SC` — Science Cruiser or Secure Cargo
- `Sc` — Science (less common)
- `D` — Destroyer
- `I` — Installation
- `T` — Transport
- `SG` — Sentry Gun
- `EP` — Escape Pod
- `A` — AWACS
- `G` — Gas Miner
- `Cv` — Corvette
- `Ff` / `Fg` — Frigate
- `J` — Juggernaut
- `M` — Medical
- `NB` — NavBuoy

Examples: `GTF` (Galactic Terran Fighter), `GTB`, `GTD`, `GVD`, `PVSC`, `PVFr`, `SCv`, `SB`.

**Note**: This convention applies to the standard FreeSpace universe. Mods set in a different universe may use entirely different naming schemes and should not be assumed to follow these prefixes.

description: Left-handed axes, meters, orientation matrices, Euler angles
text:
FreeSpace uses a **left-handed coordinate system**:

- **+X** is right
- **+Y** is up
- **+Z** is forward

A ship's nose points along the +Z axis.

**Units**: All distances are in **meters**.

**Orientations** are represented as 3x3 rotation matrices consisting of three vectors in this order:
- **rvec** (right vector)
- **uvec** (up vector)
- **fvec** (forward vector)

An identity orientation (facing forward, no rotation) is: rvec=(1,0,0) uvec=(0,1,0) fvec=(0,0,1).

**Euler angles**: heading (h) = rotation around the Y axis, bank (b) = rotation around the Z axis, pitch (p) = rotation around the X axis. Rotations are applied in the order heading, bank, pitch (i.e. Y, Z, X). Strictly speaking these are Tait-Bryan angles, not classic Euler angles.

**Model bounding boxes**: The `minimums` and `maximums` fields from `get_ship_class_model_details` define the bounding box of the ship model in the model's local coordinate frame. For example, `maximums.z - minimums.z` gives the overall length of the ship.

description: Primary, secondary, and beam weapon types
text:
FreeSpace has two types of weapon banks: primary and secondary. There are three logical categories of weapon:

- **Primary weapons** (subtype "primary"): Lasers and ballistic cannons. They fire from primary weapon banks and typically have unlimited or ammo-based ammunition.

- **Secondary weapons** (subtype "secondary"): Missiles, bombs, and torpedoes. They fire from secondary weapon banks and consume finite ammunition.

- **Beam weapons** (subtype "beam"): High-energy directed beams. Although beams are a distinct category with unique mechanics, they are always mounted on primary weapon banks because there are only two physical bank types. In the game data, most beam weapons have the internal subtype WP_LASER (not WP_BEAM) because they are defined in the #Primary Weapons section of the table files. The `is_beam` property in tool responses is the reliable way to identify them.

description: Bank mounting rules and per-class allowed weapon restrictions
text:
**Bank types**: Primary weapons and beams must be mounted on primary banks. Secondary weapons must be mounted on secondary banks. This is always enforced.

**Allowed weapons**: Each ship class can define which weapons are allowed on its banks, via a global allowed-weapons list and optional per-bank overrides. The `allowed_weapons` field lists weapons allowed in regular missions; `allowed_dogfight_weapons` lists weapons allowed in multiplayer dogfight missions. In most cases the regular and dogfight lists are the same, but they can differ. When a bank has per-bank restrictions (`restricted` is true), those override the global list for that bank. But per-bank allowed-weapons are always a subset of global allowed-weapons.

**Enforcement**: In practice, allowed weapons are only enforced for ships that belong to wings listed in Starting Wings. For other ships, the mission designer can assign any weapon with a compatible type (primary/secondary), but assigning a non-allowed weapon is considered a violation of the in-universe rules. Even for Starting Wings, allowed weapons are not enforced if the mission is a Scramble mission.

description: Default lock states for beam and non-beam turrets; fire-beam SEXP
text:
Turret weapons can be locked or freed on a ship-wide or per-turret basis using SEXPs. Non-beam turret weapons are **freed by default** and will fire automatically. Beam weapons are **locked by default** and will not fire unless explicitly unlocked, either per-turret, ship-wide, or by using the "beam free all by default" mission flag.

Ships will intelligently and automatically fire appropriate weapons at their targets, which is sufficient for most scenarios, including most scenarios with beams. For precise control over beam combat, the `fire-beam` SEXP operator can command a specific beam turret to fire at a specific target ship and subsystem. If `fire-beam` is used, the relevant beam turret should be locked so that automatic beam firing does not overlap with specific beam firing.

description: Targetable ship components: engines, weapons, turrets, hangars
text:
Subsystems are targetable components of a ship, each with their own hitpoints. Common subsystem types include:

- **Engines**: Disabling stops the ship from moving
- **Weapons**: Disabling prevents the ship from firing
- **Sensors/Communication**: Affects targeting and messaging
- **Navigation**: Affects autopilot and warp capability
- **Turrets**: Individual gun emplacements on larger ships
- **Hangar bays**: Sometimes referred to as fighter bays, these are launch and recovery bays for carried craft

Subsystems can be individually targeted, damaged, disabled, and repaired. Many SEXPs reference subsystems by name.

description: Why subsystem names require special comparison functions
text:
**IMPORTANT:** Subsystem names must NEVER be compared using common string comparison. The game engine allows minor variations in subsystem names (such as the presence or absence of a trailing 's'), so two names that look different as strings may refer to the same subsystem.

Always use the `subsystem_names_equal` tool to check if two subsystem names match, or `subsystem_names_compare` for ordering. These tools use the engine's internal comparison function that correctly handles all allowable variations.

description: Major sections of a .fs2 mission file
text:
A FreeSpace mission file (.fs2) contains several major sections:

- **Mission Info**: Metadata (name, author, description, flags)
- **Ships**: All ships placed in the mission with positions, orientations, loadouts, AI settings, and arrival/departure conditions
- **Wings**: Groups of ships that fly together in formation
- **Events**: SEXP-driven logic (triggers and actions)
- **Goals**: Mission objectives shown to the player
- **Waypoints**: Named paths for AI navigation
- **Messages**: In-mission dialogue with sender and audio
- **Briefing**: Pre-mission briefing stages with text and camera positions
- **Debriefing**: Post-mission stages triggered by conditions
- **Reinforcements**: Ships/wings that can be called in mid-mission

FRED2 is the mission editor. Missions are saved with the .fs2 extension.

description: Canonical names (31-char keys) and player-facing display names
text:
A **name** is the canonical key for most FRED entities, including ships, ship classes, wings, jump nodes, and so forth. Names have a hard limit of 31 characters. Many entities also support a **display name**, which can be of unlimited length. If a display name is present, it is shown to the player during a mission instead of the canonical name. Names are used in SEXPs, table references, and internal lookups; display names are purely for player-facing presentation.

When a name is assigned to an entity, if the name contains a hash character `#`, a display name is automatically created from the characters preceding the hash. For example, a ship named `Corsair#2` would automatically get the display name `Corsair`. This allows multiple ships to share the same player-visible name while retaining unique canonical names. However, a double hash `##` disables the truncation — instead, the `##` is converted to a single `#` in the display name. For example, a name like `Alpha##1` would get the display name `Alpha#1`.

description: Wing formation, naming, waves, size limits, and conventions
text:
A **wing** is a named group of 1 to 6 ships that fly and fight together. Key properties:

- **Naming**: When ships are added to a wing or formed into a wing, they are renamed to the wing name followed by a space and their position number (e.g., "Alpha 1", "Alpha 2"). This is done automatically by FRED. The wing name must not conflict with any existing ship name or waypoint path name.
- **Size limit**: Most wings are limited to 6 ships, but wings in the Starting Wings list are limited to 4.
- **Standard wing names**: Friendly wings conventionally use Greek alphabet names: Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta, Epsilon, Zeta, Eta, Theta, Iota, Kappa, Lambda, Mu, Nu, Xi, Omicron, Pi, Rho, Sigma, Tau, Upsilon, Phi, Chi, Psi, Omega. Enemy wings conventionally use zodiac names: Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces, Aries, Taurus, Gemini. In the Great War era (during the events of FreeSpace 1 and shortly afterward), Shivan wings conventionally use Hindu names: Brahma, Vishnu, Shiva, Saraswati, Lakshmi, Parvati, Durga, Rama, Krishna, Gauri, Karikeya, Gayatri, Ganesh, Hanuman, Dyaus, Varuna, Indra, Surya, Savitar, Soma, Agni, Vayu, Ushas, Arjuna, Shivore. All of these are conventions, not hard requirements — any name can be used.
- **Waves**: A wing can have multiple waves, meaning the same group re-arrives after being destroyed. The number of waves, wave threshold (how many ships from the current wave must be destroyed before the next arrives), and delay between waves are configurable.
- **Shared properties**: All ships in a wing share arrival/departure conditions, initial orders, and certain flags. Individual ships can still be referenced and configured separately for properties like weapons, AI class, and ship flags.
- **Formation**: Ships in a wing fly in formation relative to the wing leader (position 1).
- **Orders**: Wings can be given orders as a group, both as initial orders and via player commands during gameplay. The wing leader's orders propagate to all wing members.

description: Starting, Squadron, and TVT wing arrays and their gameplay roles
text:
Three configurable wing name arrays control player-related wing mechanics. Each has default values that can be overridden per mission in the Mission Specs.

**Starting Wings** (default: Alpha, Beta, Gamma — min 1, max 3):
These are the wings that appear on the ship/weapon loadout screens, allowing the player to customize ship and weapon selection. (See the weapon_banks entry for information on allowed weapons.) In most types of missions, the player can be in any Starting Wing. In multiplayer team-versus-team, the Starting Wings are assigned from the TVT Wings and the player can be in any TVT Wing.

**Squadron Wings** (default: Alpha, Beta, Gamma, Delta, Epsilon — min 0, max 5):
These are the wings displayed on the HUD wingman status gauge. Fighter and bomber wings in this list show their members' status (alive, dead, departed) on the player's HUD. These can be any wings on any team. They typically include Starting Wings but are not required to.

**TVT Wings** (default: Alpha, Zeta — exactly 2):
In Team vs. Team multiplayer missions, these define which wings belong to each team. Each TVT wing corresponds to one team. When a TVT mission loads, the TVT Wings are assigned to the Starting Wings.

**Constraint**: For game engine implementation reasons, the first Starting Wing name and the first TVT Wing name must be the same (both default to Alpha). This is enforced by FRED.

**Customization**: Missions can override any of these defaults using `starting_wings`, `squadron_wings`, and `tvt_wings`.

description: Arrival cues, locations, anchors, and departure conditions for ships and wings
text:
Every ship and wing has **arrival** and **departure** settings that control when and how it enters and leaves the mission.

**Arrival cue:** A SEXP boolean condition. When the cue evaluates to true (possibly with an additional arrival delay), the ship or wing spawns into the mission. A ship may be present from mission start if the cue evaluates to true with no arrival delay.

**Arrival location:** Where the ship appears. Options are:
- **Hyperspace** (default): The ship warps in at its placed coordinates.
- **Near Ship**, **In Front of Ship**, **In Back of Ship**, **Above Ship**, **Below Ship**, **To Left of Ship**, **To Right of Ship**: The ship appears at a specified distance from an anchor ship, in the given direction. These require an arrival anchor and a positive arrival distance.
- **From Docking Bay**: The ship launches from a hangar bay on the anchor ship. The anchor ship must have hangar bay paths defined. If the ship has a fighterbay subsystem with nonzero hitpoints, such subsystem must not be destroyed.

**Arrival anchor:** The reference ship for relative-position and docking bay arrivals.

**Arrival distance:** How far from the anchor ship (in meters) for relative-position arrivals.

**Arrival delay:** Time in seconds to wait after the arrival cue becomes true before the ship actually spawns.

**Departure cue:** A SEXP boolean condition. When true (and the departure delay has elapsed), the ship initiates its departure sequence.

**Departure location:** How the ship leaves. Options are:
- **Hyperspace** (default): The ship warps out.
- **To Docking Bay**: The ship flies to and docks with the anchor ship's hangar bay before leaving.

**Departure anchor:** The target ship for docking bay departures.

**Departure delay:** Time in seconds to wait after the departure cue becomes true before departing.

**Wing vs. ship arrival/departure:** When a ship is part of a wing, the wing's arrival and departure settings are used — not the individual ship's. All ships in a wing arrive and depart together.

**Reinforcements:** Ships or wings marked as reinforcements do not arrive automatically when their arrival cue becomes true. Instead, becoming true makes the reinforcement available for the player to call in via the communications menu.

**Permanent failure:** Arrival and departure cues are subject to the same permanent true/false evaluation as event and goal conditions. If a cue becomes logically impossible — for example, an arrival cue that checks `has-departed` on a ship that was destroyed instead — the cue is permanently marked as false and the ship will never arrive. Similarly, if a docking bay arrival's anchor ship is destroyed, the arrival can never occur. See the event_behavior note for details on how permanent evaluation works.

description: Event formulas, repeat/interval/trigger counts, and chaining
text:
Mission events use SEXP formulas as triggers and actions. A typical event has a conditional SEXP that, when true, fires one or more action SEXPs. Event formula root nodes must be an operator with return type 'void' (an action operator like `when`, `when-argument`, or `do-nothing`). The most common event pattern is `(when <condition> <action1> <action2> ...)`.

**repeat_count**: How many times the event will be evaluated after first becoming true. Default is 1 (evaluate once, then done). Set to -1 for infinite repeats. After each successful evaluation, the count decrements; when it reaches 0 the event is permanently done and its score is awarded.

**interval**: Time between successive re-evaluations of a repeating event, in seconds (or milliseconds if the milliseconds unit option is used). Default is 1 second. Only meaningful when repeat_count > 1 or repeat_count = -1. The event formula is NOT re-evaluated every frame — after each evaluation, the engine waits for the interval to elapse before the next one.

**trigger_count**: An optional secondary limit on how many times the event is allowed to *trigger* (succeed). Unlike repeat_count which decrements for all event evaluations, trigger_count only decrements when the event evaluates to true. This allows an event to fire on non-consecutive evaluations (e.g., true, false, true = 2 triggers out of 3 evaluations). The event stops when EITHER repeat_count or trigger_count reaches 0. Set to -1 for unlimited triggers. Only active when explicitly set (the engine tracks this with an internal flag).

**Interaction between interval and trigger_count**: When trigger_count is used with repeat_count = -1 (infinite), the interval behaves as a cooldown after each successful trigger rather than a constant polling rate. Between triggers, the event checks continuously (every evaluation cycle) to catch the moment the condition becomes true. When it triggers, the interval delay is applied before the next evaluation. After the interval elapses, continuous checking resumes. When trigger_count is used with a finite repeat_count, the interval is applied after every evaluation regardless of result, acting as a fixed polling rate.

**Event chaining**: Events can be chained to the event immediately preceding them in the event list. A chained event will not be evaluated until its predecessor has become true, and will never be evaluated if its predecessor becomes false. The **chain_delay** specifies how long to wait (in seconds or milliseconds) after the predecessor becomes true before evaluating the chained event. A chain_delay of -1 means the event is not chained (default). A chain_delay of 0 means evaluate as soon as the predecessor is true.

description: Mission goals, formula holders, and return type validation
text:
**Goals**: Mission goals are objectives shown to the player. Goal formulas must have a root operator with return type 'boolean' — the goal is satisfied when the formula evaluates to true. Goals have a type (Primary, Secondary, or Bonus) that affects how they are presented to the player.

**Other formula holders**: Fiction viewer stages and briefing/debriefing stages also use boolean SEXP formulas as visibility conditions. These determine when a stage is shown to the player.

**Formula return type validation**: When attaching a SEXP node to an entity via `create_event`, `update_event`, `create_goal`, `update_goal`, or similar tools, the formula's return type is validated automatically. Passing a boolean operator (like `true`) as an event formula, or a void operator (like `when`) as a goal formula, will be rejected with an error.

description: Directive visibility, permanent true/false, and absent ship handling
text:
**Directive visibility**: Events with `objective_text` display a directive on the player's HUD. The visibility of a directive depends on the event's condition: if the condition references specific ships or wings, the directive is automatically hidden until all referenced ships/wings have arrived. If the condition references only goals or other non-ship entities, the directive is visible from the moment the event is first evaluated, which is usually at mission start. This means unchained events that check for ship/wing destruction (e.g., "Destroy Corsair") will naturally appear when the relevant wing arrives, without needing explicit chaining. Chaining is needed when the automatic arrival-gating is insufficient — for example, return-to-base directives whose conditions reference the player ship (which is always present) must be chained to a gate event so they only appear at the appropriate moment.

**Permanent true or false**: When a condition becomes logically permanent, the event is marked done. In the case of failure, the condition will never be evaluated again, even if it has remaining repeat or trigger counts. For example, if an event checks `is-destroyed Alpha 1` but Alpha 1 departs the mission instead of being destroyed, the condition can never become true. In this case the event silently stops, its score is never awarded, and any events chained to it will never fire. The same applies to goals: a goal whose condition becomes impossible is permanently failed.

**Action operators and absent ships**: Action operators that reference ships which are absent — whether not yet arrived, already departed, or already destroyed — silently skip those ships and complete successfully without causing errors. If a flag-setting action (such as `protect-ship` or `ship-guardian`) targets a ship that has not yet arrived, the flag is applied to the ship's arrival data so it takes effect when the ship enters the mission. Actions targeting departed or destroyed ships are simply ignored. In all cases, the event continues normally and is not failed.

description: Color tags, localization tokens, key bindings, and variable substitution
text:
Mission text (briefings, debriefings, command briefings, messages, subtitles) can contain special tokens that are replaced at runtime.

**Localization tokens**:
- `$callsign` → player's callsign
- `$rank` → player's rank display name
- `$rtitle` → player's rank title (abbreviated)
- `$quote` → double quote character `"`
- `$semicolon` → semicolon `;`
- `$slash` → forward slash `/`
- `$backslash` → backslash `\`

**Briefing color tags**:
The `$` character is the meta character for color markup. Color markup can only be used in command briefings, briefings, and debriefings. Format: `$c` followed by whitespace for a color code that affects only the subsequent word (including any punctuation trailing that word), or `$c{...}$}` for a color code that affects a span of text, where `c` is a single letter indicating a color. Standard tags include `$w` (white), `$r` (red), `$g` (green), `$b` (blue), `$y` (yellow), `$o` (orange), `$f` (friendly green), `$h` (hostile red), `$n` (neutral), and uppercase variants for bright colors (`$W`, `$R`, `$G`, `$B`, `$Y`). Additional tags: `$k` (black), `$e`/`$E` (grey), `$v`/`$V` (violet), `$p`/`$P` (pink). Special sequences: `$}` closes a color span, `$|` stops coloring and can be used to prevent color from affecting trailing punctuation. Custom colors can be added via colors.tbl.

**Key remapping tokens**:
Format: `$keyname$` (with trailing `$`). Replaced with the player's actual key binding, if a control uses that key by default. Example: `$Tab$` becomes the key bound to the afterburner, whose default key is Tab.

**SEXP data substitutions**:
SEXP variable names with a leading `$` and SEXP container references with a leading `&` are replaced with their current values. Example: $result is replaced by the value of the variable `result`, if a variable with that exact name exists.

description: SEXP syntax, discovery tools, and data type constraints
text:
**Syntax**: SEXPs are the scripting language used in FreeSpace mission events. They use a Lisp-like S-expression syntax where each expression is an operator followed by its arguments in parentheses.

**Discovery tools**: SEXPs are organized into categories and subcategories. Use `list_sexp_categories` to see all categories, their subcategories, and operator counts. Use `list_sexp_operators` to list operators, optionally filtered by category, subcategory, or name. Use `get_sexp_operator` to get full details of a specific operator. Use `get_sexp_argument_type` and `get_sexp_return_type` to understand what values each argument type accepts.

**Data types**: Although strings (and data derived from strings, such as ship names and weapon names) can be accepted as arguments to SEXPs, they cannot be returned from SEXPs. SEXP return types are limited to boolean, number, and void (action operators). Furthermore, the only numbers that can be represented in the SEXP system — whether as arguments or return values — are integers. If a mission needs to work with floating-point values, this must be done in a Lua script.

description: Node links (CAR/CDR), kinds, types, and formula root asymmetry
text:
**Node navigation tools**: Use `get_sexp_node` to inspect a single node's kind, type, text, and child/sibling indices. Use `walk_sexp_tree` to get the entire subtree as a flat array with alternative index fields that can be used within that array. Use `sexp_to_text` to serialize a tree back to text.

**Node structure**: Each SEXP node has two link fields: `node_first` (first child, like LISP CAR) and `node_rest` (next sibling, like LISP CDR). Nodes have a `node_kind` ('list' or 'atom') and a `node_type` ('operator', 'number', 'string', etc.). Operator nodes also have a `return_type`.

**IMPORTANT — Formula root structure:** The root node of a formula (as stored in events, goals, etc.) is the **operator atom itself**, NOT a list node wrapping the expression. This differs from standard LISP where the root would be a list. For example, `(when (true) (do-nothing))` is stored with `when` as the root atom (node_kind='atom', node_type='operator'), and its arguments are reached via the `node_rest` chain: `when` → list(true) → list(do-nothing). However, nested sub-expressions ARE wrapped in list nodes normally — `(true)` is a list node with `node_first` pointing to the `true` atom. This asymmetry means: to get arguments of the root operator, follow `node_rest`; to get the operator inside a nested sub-expression, follow `node_first`.

description: Mission variables for storing numbers and strings in SEXPs
text:
SEXP variables allow missions to store and manipulate values (numbers and text strings) during gameplay. Each variable has a name and a default value. Variable names cannot contain spaces.

**Using variables**: The `modify-variable` SEXP operator changes a variable's value during the mission. Variables can be used as arguments in many SEXP operators. In mission text (briefings, messages, etc.), a variable's current value can be inserted by prefixing its name with `$` (e.g., `$DockCount`). See the mission_text_tokens note for details.

description: Lua scripting, dynamic SEXPs, and the scripting API discovery tools
text:
A mod can contain scripts written in Lua that provide additional functionality not coded into the game engine.

**Invocation**: Scripts can be invoked via game hooks (events fired by the engine at specific points, such as on game start, on ship death, on frame render, etc.) or evaluated by the script-eval SEXPs.

**Dynamic SEXPs**: It is also possible to call scripts from dynamic SEXPs. Dynamic SEXPs are provided by the mod rather than the game engine, by specifying SEXP operator metadata along with a script function. In terms of functionality and mission integration, they work just like built-in SEXPs.

**Discovering the API**: The scripting API is exposed through a set of focused query tools, following a list-then-drill-in pattern:
1. `list_scripting_elements` — overview of all libraries and classes (name, type, description, child count). Use optional `type` filter for just libraries or just classes, and `search` for substring matching.
2. `get_scripting_element` — full details of one library or class by name or shortName, including all its children (functions, properties, operators).
3. `search_scripting_children` — find functions, properties, or operators across all elements by name substring. Useful for answering 'where is function X?'
4. `list_scripting_hooks` — list all hooks (actions) as summaries, or get full details of one hook by name. Hooks fire at engine events and have typed hookVars.
5. `list_scripting_enums` — all enumeration constants with values.
6. `get_scripting_misc` — small sections: 'conditions' (hook condition types), 'options' (engine options), 'globalVars'.

**Element types**: `library` objects are global namespaces (e.g., `ba` for base, `mn` for mission). `class` objects are types with methods and properties (e.g., `ship`, `weapon`, `vector`). Classes can inherit via `superClass`. Each element has `children` containing its nested functions, properties, and operators.

**Hooks**: Hooks fire at specific engine events (e.g., On Ship Death, On Weapon Fired, On Frame). Each hook declares typed `hookVars` available in the script context and `conditions` for filtering. Some hooks are `overridable`, meaning a script can replace the engine's default behavior.

**Script files**: Scripts are stored as `*-sct.tbm` (table format with `#Global Hooks` and `#Conditional Hooks` sections) or `*.lua` (pure Lua files). Table-format scripts are placed in the mod's `data/tables/` directory, and pure Lua scripts are placed in the mod's `data/scripts/` directory.

**Type system in JSON**: Types can be simple strings (e.g., `"ship"`), or structured objects with a `type` field: `tuple` (multiple return values), `list` (arrays), `map` (key-value), `iterator`, `alternative` (union types), `function` (callbacks), `generic`, or `varargs`.

description: Handling modal dialog interruptions and adjusting operation timeouts
text:
FRED2 has many modal dialogs that require user input. It is possible for an MCP operation to be interrupted by a dialog, causing the operation to time out.

If an operation times out, use get_ui_status to check for modal dialogs. If a modal dialog is active, ask the user to address it before retrying the operation. If no modal dialog is active, the operation may simply need more time — use set_timeout to increase the timeout and try again.

Most operations complete quickly, in less than a second. Operations requiring a model load may take longer. Since a mission load involves loading many models, the load_mission operation typically takes the longest. The default timeout is 10 seconds.

Use get_timeout to read the current timeout value and set_timeout to change it.

description: Checking ASCII-only constraints before passing text to FRED
text: Use `get_server_info` to check the `supports_unicode` field before passing text to FRED. If `supports_unicode` is false, all strings passed to FRED — including ship names, message text, briefing text, and any other text fields — must contain only characters in the standard ASCII range [0–127]. Characters outside this range (accented letters, non-Latin scripts, emoji, etc.) must NOT be used, as they may corrupt data or cause undefined behavior.

description: How the search parameter works in list tools
text:
All list tools that accept a `search` parameter use **fuzzy matching** against names (not descriptions or other long text). The search does not require an exact substring — it tolerates missing, extra, or reordered characters, making it useful for approximate lookups when the exact name is not known.

**Ranking**: When a search is active, results are sorted by match quality (best match first) rather than the default order. Exact and near-exact matches appear at the top.

**Short queries**: Very short search strings (1-2 characters) may return many results because the fuzzy matcher is permissive with few input characters. Use 3 or more characters for more focused results.

**Scope**: The search matches against the primary name field of each item (e.g., operator name, topic name, element name, hook name, enum name). It does not search descriptions, categories, or other fields.
